content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 4, 1994

Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel, Pong, Leidigh,
Waters and Fishburn
CapitolBank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-93-472

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

You have requested advice concerning the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ Because you have not
identified the clients on whose behalf you are writing, we
consider your letter to be a request for informal assistance
pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).²

FACTS

Lobbyist A ("A") is registered to lobby for lobbying firm B
("B"). Client C ("C") retains B to represent it regarding a bill
before the Legislature. In the course of that representation, C
also asks A for assistance regarding an unrelated matter. That
matter concerns difficulty that C is having with Department D
("D"), an administrative agency within the executive branch of the
state government. The difficulty is that C perceives D to be
"dragging its feet" in resolving an issue of concern to C. You
have indicated that the issue of concern to C is not
"administrative action" within the meaning of Section 82002 and
Regulation 18202. C has asked A to request assistance from a
"legislative official" in resolving its problem with D.

_____________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Leidigh, Robert
No. I-93-472
Page 2

QUESTION

Does contact by A with a "legislative official" regarding C's
problem with D constitute activity which must be reported by
either A, B or C?

CONCLUSION

This activity is not reportable by either A, B or C.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires lobbying firms and lobbyist employers to
report payments made or received in connection with attempting to
influence "legislative action" and "administrative action."

Section 82002 defines "administrative action" as:

...the proposal, drafting development, consideration,
amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any
rule, regulation, or other action in any rate-making
proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding....

Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as:

...the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment,
report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by
either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select
committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the
Legislature acting in his official capacity. "Legislative
action" also means the action of the Governor in approving or
vetoing any bill.

According to the information in your letter, the matter which
concerns C regarding D does not meet the definitions of
"legislative action" or "administrative action." Therefore, a
payment made in connection with communications between A and a
legislative official regarding this activity need not be reported
by A, B, or C. However, if C attempts to influence
"administrative action" regarding D, payments made and received
would be reportable.

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Wayne/P. Imberi
Political Reform Consultant
